turns.content: ok i got back from the holidays and my plants look like they're staging a protest. or maybe a funeral. one of those
Oh no — how many plants and what's the damage looking like? Yellowing, drooping, crispy edges, something else? Also how long were you gone?

gone for about 11 days. i have 4 plants: a pothos, a monstera, a snake plant, and this little succulent. the pothos is super droopy, the monstera has like 2 yellow leaves, the snake plant looks fine actually, and the succulent is... mushy? which seems bad

Okay so you've got three different problems happening here, which is actually useful because they have different causes.

The pothos drooping is almost certainly just thirst — pothos are dramatic about water and will go full wilting-faint when they're dry. Check the soil: if it's bone dry a few inches down, give it a thorough watering and it should perk up within a few hours, maybe overnight. They bounce back fast. Not a crisis.

The monstera's yellow leaves: two leaves after 11 days away is probably just the plant shedding some older leaves that were already on their way out. If the yellowing is on the oldest/lowest leaves and the newer growth looks healthy, I wouldn't worry about it. If it's spreading or random, that can indicate overwatering before you left, or nutrient stuff.

The mushy succulent is the one I'd actually be concerned about — mushy usually means root rot from overwatering, not underwatering. Did you water everything before you left? What does the soil feel like now?
oh god i definitely watered everything before i left because i felt guilty about leaving them. including the succulent. probably too much tbh

Yeah that tracks — succulents really do not want to be watered before a trip. They're designed to hold water and they hate sitting in wet soil. Mushy usually means the roots are rotting.

Here's what to do: take the succulent out of its pot right now and look at the roots. Healthy roots are white or light tan and firm. Rotten roots are brown, black, and soft — they'll basically fall apart. If it's only partially rotted, you can try to save it: trim off all the mushy roots and dead stem with clean scissors, let the whole thing dry out on the counter for 2-3 days with NO soil and NO water (this sounds brutal but the cut ends need to callous over), then repot in fresh dry cactus/succulent mix. Don't water it again for at least a week after repotting.

How mushy are we talking — is it just the bottom of the stem or more widespread?
just the bottom i think... the top still looks ok and has color. is that salvageable?

That's actually good news — if the top is still firm and colored, the rot hasn't traveled up yet. You have a real shot at saving it with the cut-and-dry method. The key is to cut below the mushy part into healthy firm tissue — even if you have to cut pretty far up. The healthy part is what matters and you can propagate it as a new plant even if you lose the root system entirely. Succulents are weirdly resilient once you get past the rot.

Go do it now while it's early. Every day you wait the rot can spread upward.

ok i did it, cut below the mushy part, the cut end looks healthy. now it's just sitting on my counter looking kind of sorry for itself. also the pothos already perked up after watering which is wild, i watered it like 20 minutes ago

Pothos honestly have the fastest recovery of any plant — they're pure drama, completely fine, no in-between. It was probably fine the whole time it was wilting and just needed to be noticed.

For the succulent: now you wait. I know it looks sad sitting there bare but the callous-over process is actually important. Keep it somewhere bright but out of direct harsh sun while it's healing — the exposed cut tissue is vulnerable. After 2-3 days you can pot it up. And then seriously — no water for at least a week, maybe two. The roots it grows in the new soil will be fine without it.

While you were gone, was the apartment heated normally? Sometimes plants stress over the holidays because people turn the heat way down and the temperature drop causes more issues than the missed watering.
oh. hm. i turned the heat down to like 62 when i left to save on the bill. could that have stressed the monstera?

62 for 11 days — possibly, yeah. Monsteras are tropical plants and they prefer 65-85°F. They can tolerate brief dips below 60 but prolonged cold stresses them and can cause yellowing. Combine that with dry soil and you've got a stressed monstera. The good news is that two yellow leaves is a pretty mild reaction — it's basically the plant telling you what happened, not a sign of serious damage.

Going forward: if you leave for more than a few days in winter, try to keep the heat at least at 65 for the plants' sake. Or consider one of those self-watering globes — they're not perfect but they work reasonably well for pothos and monstera to keep the soil from going completely bone dry.
the self watering globe things, do those actually work or are they gimmicky? i've seen them at target and always assumed they were garbage

They work okay for what they're designed to do, which is slow-release moisture over 1-2 weeks. They're not magic and they're not a substitute for actual care, but for a planned absence they're genuinely useful for thirsty plants like pothos. You fill the glass bulb with water, jam the spike into the soil, and capillary action slowly releases water as the soil dries out. For 10-11 days on a pothos or monstera, one or two of them would probably have kept your plants comfortable.

The catch is that the flow rate depends on the soil density and you have to experiment a bit to figure out how fast yours drain. And obviously do NOT use one on the succulent. But for your tropical plants? They're worth the $5-8 at Target, not gimmicky.
ok adding to my target list. the snake plant is really out here just not caring about any of this drama huh

Snake plants are basically indestructible — they actively prefer being ignored. Infrequent watering, variable temperatures, low light, doesn't care. There's a running joke in plant circles that if you can kill a snake plant you might have a gift. Yours just watched the whole crisis unfold and did absolutely nothing because it did not need to. Aspirational honestly.
i aspire to be as unbothered as my snake plant. ok update in a few days on whether the succulent survived
Rooting for it — pun somewhat intended. The early cut was the right move, so it's got a good shot. Welcome back from the holidays.
